purpose: Complete theory graph: all psychological theories, paradoxes and cognitive biases decomposed into official BCIO/MeSH/GO term clusters with DREFS module assignments, variability through ontology hierarchy.
principle: Theory T = core_cluster (required) + full_cluster (extended) + DREFS module. Activates when core_cluster found in text. Variability: parent/sibling/children substitute at reduced confidence. Paradoxes = failure_cluster (gap detection). Biases = MoA subtype within Engagement/Dose.

types.paradox: failure_cluster: conditions where behaviour logic breaks
types.bias: MoA subtype: systematic deviation in processing

DOS:000001.label: Mere Exposure Effect
DOS:000001.gap_pattern: Mere Exposure Effect absent: text does not report exposure frequency or repetition count. Dose module cannot distinguish novelty from familiarity effects. Engagement through repeated exposure is undetectable.

siblings.label: history of exposure to an occupational hazard

DOS:000001.notes: Repeated exposure via associative learning + intervention exposure
DOS:000001.definition: Repeated exposure to a stimulus tends to increase liking for it, even without deep processing or explicit awareness. Familiarity alone can foster preference.

ENG:000002.label: ABC Model / REBT
ENG:000002.gap_pattern: ABC Model / REBT absent: text does not report irrational beliefs mediating between activating event and consequence. B-layer (belief filter) uncharacterised — emotional and behavioural consequences appear stimulus-driven.
ENG:000002.definition: A model from Rational–Emotive Behaviour Therapy stating that emotions and behaviours are not determined by events themselves but by the beliefs and interpretations people hold about those events. Changing irrational beliefs can therefore change emotional reactions and actions.

TG:ABC023.notes: HAPA two-phase model. Motivational phase: risk perception (BCIO:050607.00.01.ma.r) + outcome expectancy (BCIO:006060.02.03.ma.e) + motivational self-efficacy (BCIO:006154.04.02.ma.e) -> intention. Volitional phase: action planning (BCIO:007010.00.17.bc.f) + coping self-efficacy (BCIO:006044.02.03.ma.e) + schedule (BCIO:009000.00.03.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.sh) + accountability (BCIO:050963.00.01.ma.f) -> behaviour.
TG:ABC023.definition: Divides behaviour change into a motivational phase (forming intention) and a volitional phase (planning and action). It emphasises action and coping planning and self‑efficacy in maintaining change.

TG:NEW003.notes: Deictic stimuli (pointing gestures, gaze direction) trigger automatic attentional capture (100-300ms, pre-conscious) via STS/IPS network. Three effects: (1) attentional capture -> BCIO:050785.00.00.ma.e + MF:0000018.02.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.e; (2) intentionality attribution -> MF:0000088.00.38.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d (subliminal) + MF:0000019.00.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d; (3) pedagogical stance (Csibra & Gergely) -> reduced criticality. Dose: brief exposure to deictic cue redirects attention without conscious processing. Universal across culture/age/SES - operates at Shepard gradient d=0.

TG:NEW004.notes: Evolutionary mechanism: ostensive deictic signals trigger learning mode - reduced criticality + increased acceptance + information treated as generalizable. TG:NEW006.label: Information Cascade

TG:NEW006.notes: Sequential choice phenomenon: individuals adopt decisions by observing predecessors rather than own information. Cascade mechanism: sequential choice (050488 intervention exposure) -> collective behaviour intention (050681) -> social learning (050950) -> tipping point at critical mass (~16-25% population). Information pie model: Fact -> Interpretation -> Rumour -> Counter-narrative. Reach: cascade spreads independently of information quality. Key BCT: credible source (BCIO:006142.00.02.ma.r.f) to break negative cascade. Links to Diffusion of Innovations S-curve and Tipping Point Theory.
TG:NEW006.definition: Describes situations in which people disregard their own information and follow the decisions of others, leading to behavioural cascades. Early movers can disproportionately shape group outcomes.

TG:BIAS030.label: Wishful Thinking
TG:BIAS030.author: Bastardi et al.
TG:BIAS030.gap_pattern: Desired outcome activates associative priming (MF:0000088.00.38.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d + BCIO:006126.00.04.ma.d) which biases information search and evaluation toward confirming evidence. Failure: risk of bias (020000) is systematically underestimated when outcome is wished for; source credibility (006142) inflated for confirming sources; self-monitoring (007017) selectively attends to positive signals; accountability belief (050963) relaxed — motivated reasoning replaces critical evaluation. In behaviour change context: wishful thinking about intervention efficacy produces overconfident dose estimates and fidelity blindspots.
TG:BIAS030.notes: Wishful thinking = motivated cognition: desired outcome primes confirmatory associative memory (BCIO:006126.00.04.ma.d) via subliminal activation (MF:0000088.00.38.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d). Three mechanisms: (1) associative transfer — logo/brand primes desired qualities below conscious threshold; (2) selective information search — attention directed to confirming evidence (BCIO:050785.00.00.ma.e distorted); (3) motivated reasoning — belief about gain (BCIO:006023.00.31.ma.e) overrides evidence-based evaluation. Failure cluster activates when wishful thinking corrupts: measurement fidelity (BCIO:020000.00.07.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.f), source assessment (BCIO:006142.00.02.ma.r.f), self-monitoring (BCIO:007017.08.19.bc.f), accountability (BCIO:050963.00.01.ma.f).

TG:NEW008.notes: PCT: organisms control perceptions, not behaviours. Behaviour is varied as a means to bring perception to a reference value. Hierarchy of perception levels: lower levels control sensory variables, higher levels set reference signals for lower levels. Learning = reorganisation of reference hierarchy (Schedule). Link to ABC (Ellis): B (belief) is the reference value; A is the disturbance; C is the control output to reduce discrepancy. PCT extends ABC: the filter (B) is itself learnable and hierarchically organised. Volitional control of perceptual filter = training higher-level reference values to override lower automatic ones (self-monitoring BCIO:006137.00.01.ma + monitoring BCT BCIO:007017.08.19.bc.f). Self-efficacy (BCIO:006060.02.03.ma.e) = confidence that control loop can close the gap. Willingness to change (BCIO:050952.02.11.ma.e) = readiness to reorganise reference hierarchy. Powers 1973: Behavior: The Control of Perception. Aldine de Gruyter.
TG:NEW008.definition: Treats behaviour as the active control of perception through hierarchies of feedback loops, rather than passive reaction to external stimuli. # Additional Theories and Concepts

TG:LAW007.definition: When different explanations fit the data equally well, the simpler one—requiring fewer entities and assumptions—should be preferred. It is a heuristic for model choice, not a strict logical law.

TG:LAW010.author: Tesler

TG:DEC001.label: Dual Process Theory (System 1 / System 2)
TG:DEC001.author: Kahneman/Evans
TG:DEC001.gap_pattern: Dual Process Theory absent: automatic vs deliberate processing channel targeted by intervention unspecified. Intervention targeting System 2 while behaviour driven by System 1.
TG:DEC001.notes: System 1: fast, automatic, associative (MF:0000088.00.38.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d subliminal + 006125 operant). System 2: slow, deliberate, effortful (006116 decision-making). Dose determines which system activates. Bridge: Reflective-Impulsive Model.
TG:DEC001.definition: Proposes two modes of processing: a fast, automatic, intuitive system (System 1) and a slow, deliberate, analytical system (System 2). Everyday life is dominated by System 1, with System 2 engaged for complex tasks.

TG:NEU011.label: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
TG:NEU011.author: DSM-5 / APA
TG:NEU011.gap_pattern: PTSD mechanisms absent from intervention text: hyperarousal (MF:0000012.00.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d.sh) and spontaneous recovery (BCIO:050838.00.14.ma.sh) not detected. What is invisible: (1) trauma cue sensitization as Dose mechanism — intervention dose may inadvertently re-expose to trauma cues below conscious threshold (MF:0000088.00.38.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d); (2) avoidance as Engagement barrier — motivation to deviate (BCIO:050947.00.08.ma.e) driven by trauma-conditioned fear rather than disengagement; (3) extinction failure as Schedule gap — spaced exposure required for habituation but fear spontaneously recovers (BCIO:050838.00.14.ma.sh) if schedule is interrupted; (4) Fidelity risk — trauma-focused protocols require strict delivery fidelity (BCIO:007017.08.19.bc.f) to prevent iatrogenic re-traumatisation.
TG:NEU011.definition: Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder is a psychiatric condition triggered by exposure to actual or threatened death, serious injury or sexual violence. Characterised by four symptom clusters: intrusive re-experiencing (flashbacks, nightmares), persistent avoidance of trauma-related stimuli, negative alterations in cognition and mood, and marked alterations in arousal and reactivity (hypervigilance, exaggerated startle response, sleep disturbance). Symptoms persist beyond one month and cause significant functional impairment. Core mechanisms: failure of fear extinction (GO:0046959.00.01.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d habituation failure), conditioned sensitization to trauma cues (GO:0046960.00.01.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d), subliminal trigger processing (MF:0000088.00.38.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d), spontaneous recovery of extinguished fear responses (BCIO:050838.00.14.ma.sh), and chronic hyperarousal (MF:0000012.00.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d.sh). Evidence-based interventions: Prolonged Exposure (PE), Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), EMDR.

TG:NEU011.notes: PTSD as DREFS construct: Dose — trauma exposure history (BCIO:050488.00.09.be.p.ma.sd.d.r) + cue dose activating subliminal fear response (MF:0000088.00.38.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d + GO:0046960.00.01.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d sensitization). Engagement — avoidance motivation (BCIO:050947.00.08.ma.e) competes with treatment engagement (BCIO:013000.00.00.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.e); affective appraisal distortion (MFOEM:000002.01.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.e). Schedule — extinction learning requires distributed practice (MeSH:D004304.00.00.bc.ma.sh); spontaneous recovery (BCIO:050838.00.14.ma.sh) between sessions if gaps too long; circadian arousal variation (GO:0007623.00.00.ma.se.sh + MF:0000012.00.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d.sh) affects symptom timing. Fidelity — strict protocol adherence critical: deviation from exposure hierarchy risks re-traumatisation; monitoring BCT (BCIO:007017.08.19.bc.f) required throughout. DSM-5 criteria: A=trauma exposure, B=intrusion, C=avoidance, D=negative cognitions/mood, E=hyperarousal.

TG:NEU012.label: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)
TG:NEU012.author: DSM-5 / APA
TG:NEU012.gap_pattern: ADHD mechanisms absent from intervention text: inattention (BCIO:050227.00.27.ma.d.e) and impulsivity (BCIO:050947.00.08.ma.e) not detected. What is invisible: (1) Dose — session length and information density must be calibrated to attentional capacity; cognitive overload (BCIO:050227.00.27.ma.d.e) produces disengagement not resistance; (2) Engagement — interest-based engagement (BCIO:006136.00.02.ma.e intrinsic motivation) enables hyperfocus; extrinsic motivation (BCIO:006307.01.08.ma.e) + immediate consequence timing critical for impulsivity cluster; (3) Schedule — frequent short sessions outperform infrequent long; habit formation (BCIO:006309.00.06.ma.sh) requires more repetitions than neurotypical; extreme present-bias (BCIO:050231.00.01.ma.sh) means delayed outcomes have near-zero motivational value; (4) Fidelity — external monitoring (BCIO:007017.08.19.bc.f) and accountability (BCIO:050963.00.01.ma.f) compensate for self-monitoring deficit; protocol complexity (BCIO:050839.00.03.be.ma.sd.f task complexity) must be minimised.
TG:NEU012.definition: Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder is a neurodevelopmental condition characterised by persistent patterns of inattention, hyperactivity and impulsivity that interfere with functioning across settings. Three presentations: predominantly inattentive (ADHD-I), predominantly hyperactive-impulsive (ADHD-HI), and combined (ADHD-C). Core neurobiological mechanism: dysregulation of dopaminergic and noradrenergic systems affecting prefrontal cortex function — executive function, working memory, inhibitory control and delay aversion. Inattention cluster: difficulty sustaining attention (MF:0000018.02.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.e attending + BCIO:050785.00.00.ma.e strength of attention), disengagement under cognitive load (BCIO:050227.00.27.ma.d.e), failure to complete tasks (BCIO:007002.04.17.bc.sh goal setting deficit). Hyperactivity-impulsivity cluster: motivation to deviate (BCIO:050947.00.08.ma.e), immediate reward preference (BCIO:050966.00.00.ma.d hedonic goal + BCIO:050231.00.01.ma.sh temporal orientation to present). Evidence-based interventions: stimulant medication (methylphenidate, amphetamines), behavioural parent training, cognitive-behavioural therapy, environmental modifications.

BCIO:050963.00.01.ma.f.label: belief about accountability for the consequences of an action

TG:NEU012.notes: ADHD as DREFS construct: Dose — calibrate information dose to attentional window; stimulant medication dose affects all DREFS modules simultaneously (normalises arousal MF:0000012.00.33.bc.be.p.ma.md.se.so.sd.d.sh, reduces impulsivity, extends attention). Engagement — two pathways: (a) interest/novelty activates intrinsic motivation (BCIO:006136.00.02.ma.e) enabling hyperfocus — exploit this; (b) non-interesting tasks require immediate external reinforcement (BCIO:007252.08.01.bc.e positive consequence BCT) with minimal delay. Schedule — distributed practice (MeSH:D004304.00.00.bc.ma.sh) essential; context-specific repetition (BCIO:007096.00.07.bc.sh) builds habit despite habit learning deficit (BCIO:006309.00.06.ma.sh). Fidelity — self-monitoring impairment means implementer monitoring (BCIO:007017.08.19.bc.f) must substitute; structured communication (BCIO:044120.00.50.sd.f) and environmental cues (BCIO:007081.00.06.bc.f cue BCT) scaffold adherence. DSM-5 criteria: 5+ inattention OR 5+ hyperactivity-impulsivity symptoms for 6+ months in 2+ settings, onset before age 12.

APE:000001.label: APEASE Framework
APE:000001.author: Michie S et al.
APE:000001.gap_pattern: APEASE absent: text does not report pre-launch feasibility assessment across six criteria (Acceptability, Practicability, Effectiveness, Affordability, Side-effects, Equity). Intervention may be technically sound but structurally non-viable. Launch decision made without evidence of population acceptance, resource feasibility, or equity of access.

taxonomy.direction_1: BPMS→DREFS→APEASE: analysis complete → filter → launch decision
taxonomy.direction_2: APEASE→BPMS→DREFS: criterion failure → redesign → new forecast → next wave

APE:000001.notes: APEASE operationalises six criteria for intervention decision-making. Each criterion maps to BCIO terms in APEASE_REFERENCE.json. Not a behaviour change theory per se — a meta-framework for intervention viability assessment.

_step6_patch: terms[] embedded per entry. Sources: label, gap_pattern, core_cluster.
_meta_drefs_hierarchy: DREFS_HIERARCHY v3.0 sync. Contains L1/L2/L3 internal hierarchy for all 5 DREFS modules (224 unique IDs, 260 entries with cross-module overlaps). Sources: bcio_index.json + BCIO_CANDIDATE_RANKER.json + bcio_clean.json. Use for: module activation (L1 anchor), mechanism identification (L2), specificity (L3), gap detection by level, top-down and bottom-up traversal.
